text: Welcome to another video where we'll be talking about robotic hernia surgery. Today we're going to be talking about a ETEP retrorectus in a common presentation, women following c-section. This is a forty-year-old woman who presented with a history of laparoscopic appendectomy and a c-section. She had hernias both at her peri-umbilical port site as well as in the lower abdomen related to her c-section. Review of cross-sectional imaging demonstrated two hernias and a diastasis.

I contemplated a preperitoneal repair, but as we'll see during the case, I believe that C-section incisions oftentimes have a lot of challenges with preperitoneal repair, and as such we wanted to do a retromuscular repair. Case setup plan for a lateral dock as she had a nice wide rectus space with a low threshold to convert to an upper dock if there was difficulty with dissection or defect closure in the lower abdomen. Starting at this point when we've already placed our lateral ports.

The surgeon completes the left-sided retro-rectus dissection and begins a crossover dissection in the upper abdomen. The dissection extends beyond the defect margins to ensure broad mesh coverage, reaching the patient's right semilunar line. The surgeon incises the sheath near the peri-umbilical hernia, pulling down the hernia sac and peritoneum away from the pseudosac.

The surgeon encounters a second hernia defect approximately three to five centimeters below the umbilicus, related to a previous C-section. The omentum is visibly incarcerated within the hernia, and careful dissection is performed to separate it while maintaining clear visualization to prevent injury to surrounding structures. The neck of the defect is visible, along with the incision from the prior C-section.

The video shows the anterior sheath largely intact, while the posterior sheath remains open. The limited tissue available for a preperitoneal repair is noted, highlighting the benefits of a wide dissection for retromuscular repair to address the hernia defects effectively.

The surgeon extends the dissection downwards by approximately five centimeters to ensure adequate coverage of the multifocal defects and diastasis. A larger hernia is visible, which is addressed using a retromuscular approach. The process of closing and placating the midline begins, with the posterior sheath being incised and the fascia placated for reinforcement. Mesh coverage is applied, extending to the upper portions of the dissection.

The surgeon uses a number one permanent V-lock suture to placate the lower abdomen, achieving approximately ten centimeters of placation before reaching the peri-umbilical defect. The muscle bellies are re-approximated, and the posterior aspect remains open. The decision to close the anterior or posterior first is noted as a matter of personal preference, given the small size of the hernia.

The surgeon completes the midline closure and focuses on closing the posterior visceral sac without re-approximating the sheaths to avoid tension. The visceral sac is closed to prevent mesh interaction with intraabdominal contents. A macroporous polypropylene mesh, measuring 23 cm wide by 30 cm long, is strategically rolled and inserted through an 8 mm trocar, then unrolled to reach the ipsilateral semilunar line. The mesh is fixated using Tissio spray, with no drain placed due to limited bleeding.
Uncomplicated post-op course with a same-day discharge. Patient has done well in the initial post-op period. Thank you very much. I hope that was educational.
